A shuffled deck of 52 cards is almost certainly in an order no deck has ever been in before, anywhere in history.
There are more ways to arrange a deck of cards than there are atoms in planet Earth.
In a room of just 23 people, there is a better than 50 percent chance that two of them share a birthday.
Euclid proved there are infinitely many prime numbers over 2,000 years ago, and his proof still holds up today.
The largest known prime number has more than 41 million digits. Printed in a book, it would fill thousands of pages.
Zero point nine repeating is not almost one. It is exactly, precisely equal to one.
A million seconds is about 11 and a half days. A billion seconds is nearly 32 years.
Pi has been computed to over 100 trillion digits, yet NASA needs only about 15 of them to navigate the solar system.
A googol is a one followed by 100 zeros, more than the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe.
You could never write out a googolplex in full. The universe does not contain enough matter to hold all its digits.
Graham's number is too large to write even with towers of exponents, yet mathematicians know its last digit is 7.
In the Monty Hall puzzle, switching doors wins two thirds of the time. Thousands of readers, some with doctorates, wrote in to insist that was wrong.
Real-world numbers like invoices and populations start with the digit 1 about 30 percent of the time. Investigators use the pattern to catch fraud.
There are more possible games of chess than there are atoms in the observable universe.
Some infinities are bigger than others. Cantor proved the real numbers strictly outnumber the counting numbers.
There are exactly as many even numbers as there are whole numbers. Infinity refuses to play by everyday rules.
Six is a perfect number, equal to the sum of its divisors 1, 2, and 3. The next one is 28, and after that 496.
Nobody has ever found an odd perfect number, and nobody has proved one cannot exist. The puzzle is over 2,000 years old.
Every even number ever checked, past 4 quintillion, is the sum of two primes. Proving it always works has stumped everyone since 1742.
Take any number, halve it if even, triple it and add one if odd, then repeat. Every number ever tested reaches 1, but nobody can prove they all do.
Fermat's Last Theorem went unproven for 358 years until Andrew Wiles finally cracked it after seven years of secret work.
Ramanujan instantly recognized 1,729 as the smallest number that is the sum of two cubes in two different ways.
A pizza with radius z and thickness a has a volume of pi times z times z times a. The formula spells pizza.
Seven riffle shuffles are enough to properly randomize a deck of cards. Fewer than that leaves detectable order.
Measure a coastline with a shorter ruler and it gets longer. Britain's coast has no single true length.
Any flat map can be colored with just four colors so that no neighboring regions match. The proof needed a computer to check nearly 2,000 cases.
Multiply any whole number by 9 and the digits of the answer always add up to a multiple of 9.
Multiply 111,111,111 by itself and you get 12,345,678,987,654,321. The digits climb to 9 and march back down.
Multiply 142,857 by any number from 2 to 6 and you get the same six digits rotated in order. It is called a cyclic number.
Take a four-digit number with different digits, sort them high minus low, and repeat. Within seven steps you always reach 6,174.
Pick two whole numbers at random and there is about a 61 percent chance they share no common factor. Pi is hiding inside that probability.
Euler's identity links e, pi, the imaginary unit, one, and zero in a single short equation. Polls of mathematicians call it the most beautiful ever.
Zero as a genuine number, with rules for arithmetic, was worked out in India around the 7th century. Europe resisted it for centuries.
Well into the 1700s, respected European mathematicians dismissed negative numbers as absurd and avoided writing them.
If you could fold a piece of paper in half 42 times, the stack would reach past the Moon.
Place one grain of rice on a chessboard square and double it each square. The final square alone needs over 9 quintillion grains.
There are prime-free gaps as long as you like. Somewhere on the number line, a billion numbers in a row contain no prime at all.
In 2013, an unknown lecturer named Yitang Zhang stunned the field by proving infinitely many primes sit within 70 million of each other.
A drunk wanderer on a flat plane eventually stumbles home with absolute certainty. A drunk bird in three dimensions may be lost forever.
Gabriel's horn has finite volume but infinite surface area. You could fill it with paint yet never have enough to coat the outside.
The Banach Tarski theorem says a mathematical ball can be split into five pieces and reassembled into two balls identical to the original.
A completely full hotel with infinitely many rooms can still take a new guest. Everyone just moves up one room.
Adding 1 plus a half plus a third and so on eventually passes any number, but just reaching 100 takes about 10 to the power 43 terms.
Interviewing candidates one by one, the best strategy is to reject the first 37 percent, then hire the next one better than all of them.
Stuff letters into envelopes at random and the chance no letter is in the right envelope is about 37 percent, no matter how many letters.
By the pigeonhole principle, at least two non-bald people in London have exactly the same number of hairs on their head.
Internet encryption rests on an imbalance. Multiplying two huge primes is easy, but factoring the result back apart is brutally hard.
Periodical cicadas surface every 13 or 17 years. Both are primes, which may keep them from syncing with predators on shorter cycles.
Bees build hexagons for good reason. In 1999 a mathematician proved the honeycomb divides a surface into equal cells with the least wall.
Newton argued only 12 spheres can touch a central sphere of the same size. He was right, but the proof took until 1953.
In 2016, Maryna Viazovska solved sphere packing in dimension 8, then dimension 24 within a week. She won the Fields Medal for it.
Eleven plus two is an anagram of twelve plus one, and both sums equal 13.
Every odd number in English has the letter e somewhere in its name. Try to find one without it.
Ten factorial seconds, that is 10 times 9 times 8 and so on down to 1, comes to exactly six weeks.
Two Cambridge physicists once proved there are exactly 85 different ways to tie a necktie.
A Rubik's cube has about 43 quintillion possible positions, yet every one of them can be solved in 20 moves or fewer.
A Sudoku puzzle needs at least 17 starting clues for a unique solution. Ruling out 16 took a computer search of staggering size.
The digit zero does not appear in pi until the 32nd decimal place, long after every other digit has shown up.
Starting at the 762nd decimal of pi, the digit 9 appears six times in a row. The spot is nicknamed the Feynman point.
Almost every real number provably contains every possible digit pattern, yet no one has managed to prove that pi does.
In most computers, 0.1 plus 0.2 does not exactly equal 0.3. Binary machines cannot store one tenth precisely, so tiny errors creep in.
In a lottery where you pick 6 numbers from 49, the chance of hitting the jackpot is exactly 1 in 13,983,816.
At Monte Carlo in 1913, roulette came up black 26 times in a row. Fortunes were lost betting red was due. The wheel has no memory.
A flipped coin is not quite fifty fifty. It lands on the same side it started on about 51 percent of the time.
The old rule that paper cannot fold in half more than seven times fell in 2002, when student Britney Gallivan managed 12 folds.
Belphegor's prime is a palindrome reading 1, then 13 zeros, then 666, then 13 more zeros, then 1. It really is prime.
73 is the 21st prime, its mirror 37 is the 12th prime, and 21 is 7 times 3. Fans call it the Sheldon prime.
Every whole number is the sum of at most nine cubes, and only two numbers, 23 and 239, actually need all nine.
Pi was proved transcendental in 1882, finally killing the ancient dream of squaring the circle with compass and straightedge.
The square root of 2 was the first number ever shown to be irrational, a discovery that shook ancient Greek mathematics.
Write the counting numbers in a spiral and mark the primes, and they cluster along diagonals. Ulam noticed while doodling in a dull meeting.
The Riemann hypothesis has been open since 1859. Settle it and the Clay Institute will hand you one million dollars.
Of the seven Millennium Prize problems, only one is solved. Perelman proved the Poincare conjecture, then declined the million dollars.
Nobody knows whether every problem whose answer is quick to check is also quick to solve. That is P versus NP, the biggest question in computing.
There is no Nobel Prize in mathematics. The field's top honors are the Fields Medal and the Abel Prize.
The Fields Medal, mathematics' most famous prize, is only ever awarded to people under the age of 40.
Ramanujan taught himself from a borrowed textbook, then filled notebooks with thousands of results researchers are still mining today.
Paul Erdos wrote about 1,500 papers with over 500 collaborators. Mathematicians measure their distance from him with an Erdos number.
Bill Gates's only academic math paper is about sorting pancakes by flipping them, and its record stood for about 30 years.
Google is named after a spelling mistake. The founders meant googol, a one followed by 100 zeros.
Babylonians counted in base 60 about 4,000 years ago. That is why an hour has 60 minutes and a circle has 360 degrees.
The equals sign was invented in 1557 by Robert Recorde, who chose twin parallel lines because no two things can be more equal.
One famous 1966 math paper is only two sentences long. It disproves a conjecture of Euler and is still cited.
The happy ending problem earned its name because working on it led two of the mathematicians involved to marry each other.
TREE of 3, born from a simple game about drawing trees, dwarfs Graham's number so utterly the comparison barely means anything.
Zero is an even number. Mathematicians are unanimous, even though surveys show plenty of people hesitate to say so.
A4 paper's sides are in the ratio of the square root of 2, so folding a sheet in half preserves its exact proportions.
Count the spirals in a sunflower head and you usually get consecutive Fibonacci numbers, like 34 one way and 55 the other.
Fibonacci's greatest gift to Europe was not his sequence. His 1202 book introduced the Hindu Arabic numerals we use today.
Consecutive Fibonacci numbers convert miles to kilometers almost perfectly. 5 miles is about 8 kilometers, and 8 miles is about 13.
A casino's edge at roulette is only about 5 percent, yet the law of large numbers makes its long-run profit a certainty.
Given unlimited time, a monkey typing at random would eventually produce Shakespeare. That is the infinite monkey theorem, and it is provable.
Conway's Game of Life runs on a handful of simple rules, yet it can simulate any computer program ever written.
Godel proved that any consistent system of mathematics contains true statements it can never prove. Math has permanent blind spots.
In 2024, an online collaboration proved the fifth busy beaver number is exactly 47,176,870. The sixth is already incomprehensibly larger.
Add up all the numbers on a roulette wheel and the total comes to exactly 666.
You cannot comb the hair on a sphere flat without leaving a cowlick. One consequence is that somewhere on Earth the wind is always perfectly calm.
At any given moment, two exactly opposite points on Earth have the same temperature and the same air pressure. Topology guarantees it.
Pair 1 with 100, 2 with 99, and so on. Fifty pairs of 101 means the numbers from 1 to 100 add up to 5,050. Legend says Gauss saw it as a schoolboy.
Spell out every number from one up to nine hundred ninety nine and you will never write the letter A. It first shows up in one thousand.
Pick any number, spell it in English, count the letters, and repeat. You always get stuck at four, the only number equal to its own letter count.
In 1897, Indiana's legislature nearly passed a bill that got pi badly wrong. A visiting math professor talked the senate out of it.
Euler invented graph theory by proving you cannot stroll the seven bridges of Konigsberg crossing each bridge exactly once.
In the 100 prisoners puzzle, a loop-following strategy raises the group's survival chance from practically zero to over 30 percent.
On average your friends have more friends than you do. Popular people simply show up in more friend lists. It is called the friendship paradox.
The year 2025 was a perfect square, 45 times 45, and also the sum of the cubes of every number from 1 through 9.
Across the calendar's full 400-year cycle, the 13th of a month lands on a Friday more often than on any other day of the week.
Nontransitive dice exist where die A usually beats die B, B usually beats C, and yet C usually beats A. Choosing second is the winning move.
